CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (ternary weight decomposition and binary activation encoding in the Introduction). Notation is mostly consistent and defined (W, Mw, Cw, DI, DO, etc.) and algorithms are clearly presented. Presentation suffers from repeated/garbled text and duplicated paragraphs near the end, missing referenced figures/tables in the provided excerpt, and a few grammatical slips (e.g. "an another"), which prevent a top score but do not hinder overall comprehension.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper combines two existing ideas — low-rank matrix/tensor factorization for weights (citing Jaderberg et al., Zhang et al.) and integer/binary decomposition for fast inference (citing Hare et al., Ambai & Sato) — into a unified post-processing scheme (Eq.1 and Eq.2) and applies them to deep networks without retraining. The ternary-weight + binary-activation encoding and lookup-based activation quantization are practical and useful extensions, but each component is primarily an adaptation of prior techniques rather than a fundamentally new method. The clear differentiation from prior work (e.g. unlike XNOR-Net, no training change required) is noted, yet the contribution reads as a non-trivial engineering/combination advance rather than a highly novel theoretical breakthrough, so the novelty is moderate.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims (ternary weight factorization and binary activation encoding reduce memory and inference cost) are clearly stated and supported by algebraic derivations (Eqs.1–3) and a plausible greedy alternating-rank-one optimization (Alg.1) and activation encoding (Alg.2). The mathematical steps shown are consistent and there are no obvious algebraic errors; use of ternary/binary inner products via logical ops is justified by prior work cited. Experimental results on MNIST, VGG-16 and VGG-Face demonstrate large speedups and modest accuracy loss versus the original models, but the empirical methodology has shortcomings: no comparisons to other quantization/acceleration baselines (e.g. XNOR-Net/pruning), no statistical analysis or multiple runs, and some assumptions (fixed cx,bx across inputs, choice of lookup granularity L) are not extensively validated. These are minor methodological gaps that do not negate the reported benefits, so I rate it 4/5.
